title: Evaluation of Liver Function
authors: Emily D. Bethea, Daniel S. Pratt

items.title: Understanding Serum Bilirubin in Liver Disease
items.content: Serum bilirubin levels are crucial for evaluating liver function, distinguishing between unconjugated and conjugated hyperbilirubinemia to guide further investigation. Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia often indicates hemolysis or genetic conditions like Gilbert's syndrome, whereas conjugated hyperbilirubinemia suggests liver or biliary tract disease.
keyPoints: Normal total serum bilirubin: 1-1.5 mg/dL (95% of normal population between 0.2 and 0.9 mg/dL).
Isolated unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia (<15% direct) suggests hemolysis or Gilbert's syndrome.
Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia implies liver or biliary tract disease.
Bilirubinuria indicates conjugated bilirubin and suggests liver disease or obstructive jaundice.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 1)

items.title: Role of Biochemical Tests in Liver Disease Diagnosis
items.content: Biochemical tests, including serum bilirubin, aminotransferases, alkaline phosphatase, albumin, and prothrombin time, are used to detect liver disease. These tests lack specificity but can suggest the type of liver disorder (hepatocellular or cholestatic) when abnormal results persist.
keyPoints: Biochemical tests indicate general categories of liver disease.
Persistent abnormalities in multiple tests increase the probability of liver disease.
No single biochemical test assesses total liver function.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 1)

items.title: Prognostic Value of Serum Bilirubin Levels
items.content: Serum bilirubin levels are significant prognostic markers in various liver conditions. Elevated total serum bilirubin correlates with poor outcomes in viral hepatitis and alcoholic hepatitis, and is a component of the MELD score for assessing survival in end-stage liver disease.
keyPoints: Higher serum bilirubin indicates greater hepatocellular damage in viral hepatitis.
Total serum bilirubin correlates with poor outcomes in alcoholic hepatitis.
Serum bilirubin is a critical component of the MELD score for prioritizing liver transplantation.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 1)

items.title: Interpreting Aminotransferase Levels for Hepatocellular Injury
items.content: Aminotransferases, including AST and ALT, are key indicators of liver cell injury. ALT is more specific to the liver than AST, which is found in multiple tissues. Elevated aminotransferases indicate increased permeability of liver cell membranes but do not correlate with the degree of damage.
keyPoints: AST is less specific for liver injury compared to ALT.
Normal range for aminotransferases: 10-40 IU/L, though varies by lab.
Elevations up to 300 IU/L are nonspecific; >1000 IU/L suggest extensive hepatocellular injury (e.g., viral hepatitis).
AST:ALT ratio <1 in chronic viral hepatitis and nonalcoholic fatty liver disease; >2:1 suggests alcohol-related liver disease.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic Value of AST:ALT Ratios
items.content: The AST:ALT ratio provides diagnostic clues in liver disorders. A ratio <1 is typical in chronic viral hepatitis and nonalcoholic fatty liver disease, while a ratio >2:1 suggests alcohol-related liver disease.
keyPoints: AST:ALT ratio <1 indicates chronic viral hepatitis or nonalcoholic fatty liver disease.
Ratio >2:1 is suggestive of alcohol-related liver disease; >3:1 highly suggestive.
In alcohol-related liver disease, AST rarely exceeds 300 IU/L and ALT may be normal.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 2)

items.title: Elevations in Aminotransferases During Biliary Obstruction
items.content: In acute biliary obstruction, such as from gallstones, aminotransferase levels can spike to 1000-2000 IU/L but decrease rapidly, transitioning to cholestasis patterns.
keyPoints: Aminotransferases are not greatly elevated in obstructive jaundice except during acute biliary obstruction.
In such cases, aminotransferase levels can reach 1000-2000 IU/L but decrease quickly.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 2)

items.title: Interpreting Elevated Serum Alkaline Phosphatase
items.content: Elevated serum alkaline phosphatase can occur due to various conditions, including liver disease, bone disorders, and physiological changes. The source of the elevation should be identified if it's the sole abnormal finding or higher than expected.
keyPoints: Normal levels may be mildly elevated in patients over age 60 (1–1.5 times normal).
Blood types O and B can show postprandial increases after a fatty meal due to intestinal alkaline phosphatase.
Children and adolescents with rapid bone growth, and late-stage pregnancies have naturally higher levels.
Elevations less than threefold are non-specific and can occur in various liver diseases.
Elevations greater than four times normal suggest cholestatic disorders or conditions with rapid bone turnover (e.g., Paget's disease).
Fractionation by electrophoresis is precise for identifying the source of elevated alkaline phosphatase.
Serum 5'-nucleotidase or GGT measurements are practical alternatives to identify liver-origin elevations.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 3)

items.title: Clinical Implications of Alkaline Phosphatase Elevation
items.content: Elevated alkaline phosphatase without jaundice or aminotransferases suggests early cholestasis or hepatic infiltration. It is not useful for distinguishing between intrahepatic and extrahepatic cholestasis.
keyPoints: Isolated elevations may indicate primary biliary cholangitis, sclerosing cholangitis, Hodgkin's disease, diabetes, hyperthyroidism, congestive heart failure, or amyloidosis.
Elevated alkaline phosphatase of liver origin often suggests early cholestasis or hepatic infiltration by tumor or granulomata in the absence of jaundice or elevated aminotransferases.
The level of elevation does not help distinguish between intrahepatic and extrahepatic cholestasis.
Elevated values are similar across obstructive jaundice, drug-induced hepatitis, primary biliary cholangitis, sepsis, liver transplant rejection, and alcohol-induced steatohepatitis.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 3)

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS THAT MEASURE BIOSYNTHETIC FUNCTION OF THE LIVER
items.title: Serum Albumin as an Indicator of Chronic Liver Disease
items.content: Serum albumin, synthesized by hepatocytes with a long half-life (18–20 days), is not useful for detecting acute liver dysfunction due to its slow turnover. However, levels <3 g/dL should prompt consideration of chronic liver disease.
keyPoints: Serum albumin has a half-life of 18-20 days.
Minimal changes in serum albumin occur in acute liver conditions.
Albumin levels <3 g/dL suggest possible chronic liver disease.
Hypoalbuminemia can also result from protein malnutrition, nephrotic syndrome, and chronic infections.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · ■ TESTS THAT MEASURE BIOSYNTHETIC FUNCTION OF THE LIVER

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS THAT MEASURE BIOSYNTHETIC FUNCTION OF THE LIVER
items.title: Serum Globulins in Chronic Liver Disease Diagnosis
items.content: Increases in serum $\gamma$ globulins, particularly IgG >100%, are indicative of autoimmune hepatitis. Elevated IgM levels suggest primary biliary cholangitis, while increased IgA levels are associated with alcoholic liver disease.
keyPoints: $\gamma$ Globulins increase in chronic liver diseases like cirrhosis due to antibody synthesis.
IgG increases >100% may indicate autoimmune hepatitis.
Elevated IgM is common in primary biliary cholangitis.
Increased IgA levels are associated with alcoholic liver disease.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · ■ TESTS THAT MEASURE BIOSYNTHETIC FUNCTION OF THE LIVER

items.title: Understanding Hepatic Synthetic Function via Clotting Factors
items.content: The rapid turnover of clotting factors, except factor VIII which is produced by vascular endothelial cells, makes their measurement a crucial acute indicator of hepatic synthetic function. This assessment aids in diagnosing and prognosticating acute parenchymal liver disease.
keyPoints: Clotting factors are synthesized in hepatocytes with half-lives ranging from 6 hours (factor VII) to 5 days (fibrinogen).
Prothrombin time measures factors II, V, VII, and X, reflecting hepatic synthetic function.
Prolongation of prothrombin time >5 seconds above control, uncorrected by vitamin K, indicates poor prognosis in liver diseases.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · COAGULATION FACTORS

items.title: Prothrombin Time and INR in Liver Disease Prognosis
items.content: Elevated prothrombin time can indicate hepatitis, cirrhosis, or vitamin K deficiency. The INR standardizes prothrombin time across labs using the International Sensitivity Index (ISI) and is crucial for assessing liver disease severity and guiding liver transplantation decisions.
keyPoints: Prothrombin time elevation occurs in liver diseases and conditions causing vitamin K deficiency.
INR uses ISI to standardize prothrombin time, aiding in anticoagulation management and liver disease assessment.
The INR is part of the MELD 3.0 score for evaluating hepatic decompensation and organ allocation.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · COAGULATION FACTORS

items.title: Role and Limitations of Blood Ammonia in Liver Disease
items.content: Blood ammonia levels are used to detect encephalopathy or monitor hepatic synthetic function, but they have poor correlation with the presence or severity of acute encephalopathy. Elevated ammonia can occur even with normal hepatic function due to portal hypertension and shunting.
keyPoints: Poor correlation between blood ammonia levels and acute encephalopathy severity
Elevated arterial ammonia correlates with outcomes in fulminant hepatic failure
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.title: Indications for Liver Biopsy
items.content: Liver biopsy is valuable for diagnosing hepatocellular disease of uncertain cause, prolonged hepatitis with possible autoimmune involvement, unexplained hepatomegaly or splenomegaly, hepatic lesions not characterized by imaging, fever of unknown origin, and staging malignant lymphoma.
keyPoints: Useful in diffuse liver disorders
Contraindicated initially for cholestasis; assess biliary tree first
Avoid in significant ascites or prolonged INR; consider transjugular approach
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.title: Noninvasive Tests for Hepatic Fibrosis
items.content: Noninvasive tests like FibroTest, ELF test, FIB4 index, TE, and MRE are used to assess hepatic fibrosis. These methods provide alternatives to liver biopsy with varying degrees of validation across different liver diseases.
keyPoints: FibroTest has high predictive values for advanced fibrosis in chronic hepatitis C, B, alcoholic liver disease, MASLD, and methotrexate-treated psoriasis
ELF test validated for MASLD and cholestatic liver disease
TE accurate for identifying advanced fibrosis in several chronic liver diseases; FDA approved
MRE superior to TE but more costly and requires MRI access
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.subtopic: OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2) | USE OF LIVER TESTS
items.title: Role of Ultrasonography in Evaluating Liver Disease
items.content: Ultrasonography is the initial diagnostic test for patients with liver tests indicating cholestasis. It helps identify dilated biliary tracts, gallstones, and space-occupying lesions within the liver. Additionally, it distinguishes between cystic and solid masses and aids in guiding percutaneous biopsies. Doppler imaging can assess blood flow direction and patency of major hepatic vessels, crucial for diagnosing Budd-Chiari syndrome.
keyPoints: First-line test for cholestasis
Detects dilated biliary tree or gallstones
Differentiates cystic from solid masses
Guides percutaneous biopsies
Doppler imaging assesses portal vein, hepatic artery, and hepatic veins patency
First test for suspected Budd-Chiari syndrome
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2) | USE OF LIVER TESTS

items.subtopic: OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2) | USE OF LIVER TESTS
items.title: Optimizing Liver Test Interpretation for Diagnosis
items.content: To enhance the sensitivity and specificity of liver disease detection, a comprehensive panel including aminotransferases, alkaline phosphatase, bilirubin, albumin, and prothrombin time is recommended. No single test set provides a definitive diagnosis; repeated testing over days to weeks may be necessary for pattern recognition. Regional variations in etiology should be considered, with infectious diseases being more prevalent causes of liver abnormalities in developing nations.
keyPoints: Use comprehensive panel: aminotransferases, alkaline phosphatase, bilirubin, albumin, prothrombin time
No single test set provides definitive diagnosis
Repeat tests over days to weeks for pattern recognition
Regional variations in liver disease etiology
Infectious diseases more common in developing nations
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2) | USE OF LIVER TESTS

items.question: Which statement best describes a limitation of serum biochemical tests in evaluating liver function?
options: They can accurately assess the liver’s total functional capacity.
They are highly specific and sensitive for detecting liver disease.
They may be normal in patients with serious liver disease.
They provide a definitive diagnosis of specific liver disorders.
items.explanation: Serum biochemical tests lack sensitivity and specificity; they can be normal in patients with serious liver disease.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 1)

items.question: When is it most appropriate to use a battery of biochemical tests for evaluating liver function?
options: When all test results are abnormal.
To increase the sensitivity and specificity in detecting liver disease.
Only when serum bilirubin levels are elevated.
When diagnosing genetic conditions like Gilbert's syndrome.
items.explanation: Using a battery of tests increases the sensitivity and specificity for detecting liver disease.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 1)

items.question: What does an isolated elevation of unconjugated bilirubin typically indicate?
options: Hemolytic disorders or genetic conditions like Gilbert's syndrome.
items.explanation: Isolated elevation of unconjugated bilirubin is primarily seen in hemolytic disorders and genetic conditions such as Gilbert's syndrome.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 1)

items.question: What does the presence of bilirubinuria imply?
options: The presence of liver disease or obstructive jaundice.
A false-positive reading with Ictotest tablet.
items.explanation: Bilirubinuria implies the presence of liver disease or obstructive jaundice, as only conjugated bilirubin is found in urine.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 1)

items.question: Which enzyme is more specific for liver injury?
items.explanation: ALT is found primarily in the liver, making it more specific for liver injury compared to AST.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 2)

items.question: What does an AST:ALT ratio >3:1 suggest?
items.explanation: An AST:ALT ratio >3:1 is highly suggestive of alcohol-related liver disease.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 2)

items.question: Which enzyme's elevation in serum is less specific for cholestasis?
items.explanation: GGT elevation in serum is less specific for cholestasis due to its more diffuse localization.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 2)

items.question: In which condition can aminotransferases briefly reach 1000–2000 IU/L?
options: Acute phase of biliary obstruction by gallstone
items.explanation: During the acute phase of biliary obstruction caused by a gallstone, aminotransferases can briefly reach 1000–2000 IU/L.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 2)

items.question: Which condition is NOT typically associated with an isolated elevation of serum alkaline phosphatase?
items.explanation: Paget's disease is associated with bone alkaline phosphatase, not isolated liver elevation.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 3)

items.question: What is the most precise method for identifying the source of elevated serum alkaline phosphatase?
items.explanation: Electrophoresis fractionates alkaline phosphatase into its isoenzymes, identifying the source.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 3)

items.question: In which scenario does an elevated serum alkaline phosphatase suggest early cholestasis?
options: Only in patients with bone diseases
When accompanied by high levels of bilirubin
items.explanation: An isolated elevation without jaundice or elevated aminotransferases can suggest early cholestasis.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 3)

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS THAT MEASURE BIOSYNTHETIC FUNCTION OF THE LIVER
items.question: Which of the following statements about serum albumin is correct?
options: Serum albumin levels are a good indicator of acute liver dysfunction.
Serum albumin has a long half-life and reflects chronic rather than acute hepatic dysfunction.
Serum albumin should be measured routinely in all patients to screen for liver disease.
items.explanation: Serum albumin has a long half-life of 18–20 days, making it more reflective of chronic rather than acute liver dysfunction.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · ■ TESTS THAT MEASURE BIOSYNTHETIC FUNCTION OF THE LIVER

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS THAT MEASURE BIOSYNTHETIC FUNCTION OF THE LIVER
items.question: In which condition is an increase in IgM levels commonly observed?
items.explanation: Increases in the IgM levels are common in primary biliary cholangitis.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · ■ TESTS THAT MEASURE BIOSYNTHETIC FUNCTION OF THE LIVER

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS THAT MEASURE BIOSYNTHETIC FUNCTION OF THE LIVER
items.question: What is a likely cause of increased serum gamma globulin concentration in cirrhosis?
options: Increased clearance of bacterial antigens by the liver.
Increased synthesis of antibodies due to failure in clearing bacterial antigens.
items.explanation: In cirrhosis, increased serum gamma globulin concentration is due to increased antibody synthesis because the liver fails to clear bacterial antigens.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · ■ TESTS THAT MEASURE BIOSYNTHETIC FUNCTION OF THE LIVER

items.question: Which blood clotting factor's production site differs from that of other clotting factors?
options: Factor VIII is produced by vascular endothelial cells.
Factor VII is produced by hepatocytes.
Fibrinogen is produced by vascular endothelial cells.
All clotting factors are made exclusively in hepatocytes.
items.explanation: Factor VIII is the exception as it is produced by vascular endothelial cells, while others are made in hepatocytes.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · COAGULATION FACTORS

items.question: Which clotting factor has the shortest serum half-life?
options: Fibrinogen with a half-life of 5 days.
Factor VII with a half-life of 6 hours.
Factor X with a half-life of 24 hours.
Factor II with a half-life of 48 hours.
items.explanation: Factor VII has the shortest serum half-life, which is 6 hours.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · COAGULATION FACTORS

items.question: What does an elevated prothrombin time indicate in liver disease?
options: It suggests a vitamin K deficiency or liver dysfunction.
It shows decreased risk of bleeding.
It is unrelated to liver function.
items.explanation: An elevated prothrombin time can indicate disorders like hepatitis, cirrhosis, or vitamin K deficiency.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · COAGULATION FACTORS

items.question: Which test incorporates haptoglobin, bilirubin, GGT, apolipoprotein A-I, and $ \alpha_{2} $-macroglobulin for diagnosing advanced fibrosis?
items.explanation: FibroTest is the multiparameter blood test that includes haptoglobin, bilirubin, GGT, apolipoprotein A-I, and $ \alpha_{2} $-macroglobulin.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a situation where liver biopsy is considered valuable?
options: Cholestasis without prior assessment for obstruction
items.explanation: Liver biopsy should not be the initial procedure in cholestasis; biliary tree assessment is recommended first.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.question: What is a contraindication for performing a percutaneous liver biopsy?
items.explanation: Significant ascites is a contraindication for percutaneous liver biopsy.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.question: Which noninvasive test uses ultrasound waves to measure hepatic stiffness?
items.explanation: Transient elastography (TE) uses ultrasound waves to measure hepatic stiffness.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.subtopic: OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2) | USE OF LIVER TESTS
items.question: Which diagnostic test is recommended as the first step for patients with liver tests suggesting cholestasis?
items.explanation: Ultrasonography is the first diagnostic test to use in patients whose liver tests suggest cholestasis.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2) | USE OF LIVER TESTS

items.subtopic: OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2) | USE OF LIVER TESTS
items.question: What can ultrasound with Doppler imaging detect regarding blood vessels in the liver?
options: The patency and direction of blood flow in the portal vein, hepatic artery, and hepatic veins
The presence of fatty liver tissue
items.explanation: Ultrasound with Doppler imaging can detect the patency of the portal vein, hepatic artery, and hepatic veins and determine the direction of blood flow.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2) | USE OF LIVER TESTS

items.subtopic: OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2) | USE OF LIVER TESTS
items.question: In which condition is ultrasonography first ordered to detect patency and direction of blood flow?
items.explanation: Ultrasonography is the first test ordered in patients suspected of having Budd-Chiari syndrome.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2) | USE OF LIVER TESTS

items.statement: The rate-limiting step in bilirubin metabolism is the conjugation of bilirubin.
items.explanation: The rate-limiting step is the transport of conjugated bilirubin into the bile canaliculi, not its conjugation.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 1)

items.statement: Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia in an otherwise healthy patient without hemolysis requires further evaluation.
items.explanation: In the absence of hemolysis, isolated unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia can be attributed to Gilbert's syndrome and does not require further evaluation.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 1)

items.statement: Liver cell necrosis is required for the release of aminotransferases.
items.explanation: Liver cell necrosis is not required for the release of the aminotransferases.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 2)

items.statement: The normal range for aminotransferases is consistent across all laboratories.
items.explanation: There is interlaboratory variation in the normal range for aminotransferases due to technical reasons.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 2)

items.statement: The level of serum alkaline phosphatase elevation is helpful in distinguishing between intrahepatic and extrahepatic cholestasis.
items.explanation: There is no difference in values for intrahepatic vs. extrahepatic cholestasis.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 3)

items.statement: An elevated serum alkaline phosphatase greater than four times normal primarily occurs in patients with bone conditions.
items.explanation: Such elevations occur mainly in cholestatic liver disorders and infiltrative liver diseases.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 3)

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS THAT MEASURE BIOSYNTHETIC FUNCTION OF THE LIVER
items.statement: Hypoalbuminemia is specific for chronic liver disease.
items.explanation: Hypoalbuminemia can occur in conditions other than liver disease, such as protein malnutrition and nephrotic syndrome.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · ■ TESTS THAT MEASURE BIOSYNTHETIC FUNCTION OF THE LIVER

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS THAT MEASURE BIOSYNTHETIC FUNCTION OF THE LIVER
items.statement: Serum albumin levels are significantly affected by acute liver conditions.
items.explanation: Serum albumin is not a good indicator of acute liver dysfunction due to its long half-life.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · ■ TESTS THAT MEASURE BIOSYNTHETIC FUNCTION OF THE LIVER

items.statement: The INR is used to express the degree of anticoagulation on warfarin therapy.
items.explanation: INR standardizes prothrombin time and indicates anticoagulation level in warfarin therapy.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · COAGULATION FACTORS

items.statement: The MELD 3.0 score includes the serum albumin concentration.
items.explanation: MELD 3.0 score components include total serum bilirubin, creatinine, albumin, and sodium.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · COAGULATION FACTORS

items.statement: The FIB4 index is validated for use in patients with hepatitis B virus and metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease.
items.explanation: FIB4 index has been validated for hepatitis B virus and MASLD.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.statement: Blood ammonia levels are a reliable indicator of the severity of acute encephalopathy.
items.explanation: There is very poor correlation between blood ammonia levels and the presence or severity of acute encephalopathy.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.subtopic: OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2) | USE OF LIVER TESTS
items.statement: A single set of liver tests can always provide a definitive diagnosis.
items.explanation: It is often necessary to repeat these tests on several occasions over days to weeks for a diagnostic pattern to emerge.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2) | USE OF LIVER TESTS

items.subtopic: OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2) | USE OF LIVER TESTS
items.statement: Infectious diseases are more commonly the etiology of abnormal serum liver tests in developing nations than in developed nations.
items.explanation: The causes of liver test abnormalities vary according to region, with infectious diseases being more common in developing nations.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2) | USE OF LIVER TESTS

items.assertion: Elevation of the conjugated fraction of bilirubin almost always implies liver or biliary tract disease.
items.reason: The rate-limiting step in bilirubin metabolism is not conjugation but rather transport into bile canaliculi.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains why elevated conjugated bilirubin implies liver or biliary tract disease.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 1)

items.assertion: Serum biochemical tests can provide a definitive diagnosis of specific liver disorders.
items.reason: These tests suggest a general category of liver disease, such as hepatocellular or cholestatic.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because serum biochemical tests do not provide a definitive diagnosis; they only suggest a general category.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 1)

items.assertion: The absolute elevation of aminotransferases is prognostically significant in acute hepatocellular disorders.
items.reason: There is a poor correlation between the degree of liver cell damage and the level of the aminotransferases.
items.explanation: The absolute elevation of aminotransferases has no prognostic significance in acute hepatocellular disorders.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 2)

items.assertion: GGT is used to identify patients with occult alcohol use.
items.reason: Its lack of specificity makes its use questionable for identifying occult alcohol use.
items.explanation: While GGT has been advocated for this purpose, its lack of specificity makes it less reliable.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · Evaluation of Liver Function (part 2)

items.subtopic: ■ TESTS THAT MEASURE BIOSYNTHETIC FUNCTION OF THE LIVER
items.assertion: Serum albumin levels are useful for diagnosing acute liver conditions.
items.reason: Serum albumin has a long half-life and thus reflects chronic rather than acute hepatic dysfunction.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as serum albumin is not useful for diagnosing acute liver conditions due to its slow turnover.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · ■ TESTS THAT MEASURE BIOSYNTHETIC FUNCTION OF THE LIVER

items.assertion: The prothrombin time is a useful measure of hepatic synthetic function.
items.reason: It measures factors II, V, VII, and X which have rapid turnover rates.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the rapid turnover of these factors makes prothrombin time a good measure of hepatic synthetic function.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · COAGULATION FACTORS

items.assertion: Magnetic resonance elastography requires access to an MRI scanner.
items.reason: It is more expensive than transient elastography due to the need for specialized equipment.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why MRE requires an MRI scanner.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.assertion: Liver biopsy is most accurate in disorders causing focal changes throughout the liver.
items.reason: Sampling error can occur in diffuse disorders.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; liver biopsy is most accurate in disorders causing diffuse changes, not focal.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 1)

items.subtopic: OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2) | USE OF LIVER TESTS
items.assertion: Ultrasonography is the first diagnostic test to use when liver tests suggest cholestasis.
items.reason: It can detect dilated intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary tree and identify gallstones.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Evaluation of Liver Function · OTHER DIAGNOSTIC TESTS (part 2) | USE OF LIVER TESTS
